title: Epilepsy — Chronic Management
oneLiner: Recurrent unprovoked seizures require sustained antiseizure therapy; goal is seizure freedom with minimal side effects — affects ~1% of the population.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents to neurology clinic. She had her first tonic-clonic seizure six months ago, then a second last week — both occurring during sleep, witnessed by her partner. She is otherwise healthy, no fever, no head trauma. EEG shows epileptiform discharges over the right temporal lobe. MRI brain is normal. She is now starting lamotrigine. Her main concern: "Can I drive? What about pregnancy?"

**Dissect the vignette:**
- Two unprovoked seizures → diagnosis of epilepsy (≥2 seizures >24 hours apart)
- Occurring during sleep → suggests focal seizure with secondary generalisation (often temporal lobe)
- Normal MRI → no structural lesion; likely genetic/idiopathic
- EEG with temporal spikes → supports focal epilepsy
- Young woman → teratogenicity concerns guide AED choice

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Subtle focal aware seizures:** Brief déjà vu, epigastric rising sensation, or odd smell — often dismissed as "daydreaming" or anxiety
- **Nocturnal only:** Patient unaware; partner reports bizarre movements, confused arousals, or bed-wetting
- **Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy:** Morning myoclonus (jerks) in teens, often triggered by sleep deprivation — misdiagnosed as clumsiness
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Definition:** Epilepsy is a brain disorder characterised by an enduring predisposition to generate epileptic seizures, defined clinically by at least two unprovoked seizures >24 hours apart, or one unprovoked seizure with >60% recurrence risk (e.g., abnormal EEG, structural lesion). Chronic management aims to suppress seizures through long-term antiseizure medication (ASM), lifestyle modification, and monitoring for drug side effects and comorbidities.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** ~50 million people worldwide; 5–10% of the population will have a seizure in their lifetime, but only 1% develop epilepsy.
- **Stakes:** Uncontrolled seizures impair quality of life, increase risk of sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) >20-fold, and cause cognitive decline, injury, and psychiatric comorbidity.
- **Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosis (syncope, PNES), over/under-titration of ASMs, failure to address pregnancy planning, driving restrictions, and adherence issues.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:** Epilepsy results from an imbalance between excitatory (glutamate) and inhibitory (GABA) neurotransmission, often with sodium/potassium channel dysfunction. A seizure begins when a group of hyperexcitable neurons (seizure focus) synchronously depolarise and recruit surrounding tissue. In chronic epilepsy, this propensity persists due to:
- Genetic channelopathies (e.g., SCN1A mutations → Dravet syndrome)
- Scar tissue (e.g., mesial temporal sclerosis, post-stroke gliosis)
- Excitoxicity-induced remodelling (long-term potentiation)

**Why signs happen:**
- **Tonic-clonic:** Synchronous discharge → rigidity (tonic) → alternating contraction/relaxation (clonic) due to recovery cycles
- **Focal aware:** Localised cortical activation — temporal lobe → déjà vu, olfactory hallucination; motor cortex → focal jerking
- **Absence:** Thalamocortical dysfunction → brief loss of awareness without postictal confusion
- **Postictal confusion:** Exhaustion of inhibitory interneurons, neurotransmitter depletion, hyperventilation

**Connect to pattern cues:** The vignette's nocturnal seizures suggest temporal lobe origin because sleep promotes hippocampal excitability; normal MRI implies no macroscopic lesion, so pathophysiology is likely microstructural or genetic.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Cause Category | Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------------|----------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic/Genetic** | Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, childhood absence, SCN1A mutations | Onset in childhood/adolescence; normal imaging; family history |
| **Structural** | Mesial temporal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, brain tumour, stroke | Focal seizure semiology; abnormal MRI; older age at onset |
| **Infectious** | Neurocysticercosis, meningitis, encephalitis (e.g., HSV) | Fever, headache, immunocompromise; eosinophilia; travel history |
| **Autoimmune** | Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, LGI1 antibody | Subacute onset; psychiatric features, faciobrachial dystonic seizures, hyponatremia (LGI1) |
| **Metabolic/ Toxic** | Hyponatremia, hypoglycaemia, alcohol withdrawal, drug toxicity (TCAs, tramadol) | Seizure after intoxication/withdrawal; abnormal labs; acute onset |
| **Trauma** | Post-traumatic epilepsy (esp. penetrating TBI) | History of head injury; frontal lobe signs; contusion on MRI |

**Most common cause:** Despite extensive workup, ~60% remain **idiopathic** (no identifiable cause) — especially in young adults with generalised epilepsy.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (ILAE 2017):**  
- At least two unprovoked seizures >24 hours apart  
or  
- One unprovoked seizure with ≥60% probability of recurrence (e.g., epileptiform EEG, structural lesion on MRI)

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **EEG** | Interictal epileptiform discharges (sharp waves, spikes, polyspikes), background slowing | Confirm epilepsy, localise focus (focal vs generalised) | A single normal EEG does NOT rule out epilepsy; sleep-deprived EEG increases yield. |
| **MRI brain (3T, epilepsy protocol)** | Hippocampal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, tumour, stroke | Exclude structural causes | Normal MRI ≠ no epilepsy; avoid CT unless emergency. |
| **Bloods** | Glucose, Na, Ca, Mg, LFTs, U&Es, prolactin (post-ictal) | Rule out provoked seizure | Prolactin elevated >2x baseline within 20 min of seizure (supportive, not diagnostic) |
| **ECG** | Long QT, pre-excitation, arrhythmia | Differentiate syncope from seizure | Consider 12-lead routinely; tilt-table testing if uncertain. |

**Single finding that confirms:** Two or more seizures with clear epileptiform discharges on EEG → definite epilepsy.  
**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of any clinical seizure and normal EEG/imaging after full workup → consider non-epileptic attack disorder.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
(Note: "Immediate" here refers to initiating ASM after diagnosis; "Ward" refers to titration/monitoring in outpatient clinic; "Long-term" is maintenance and lifestyle.)

**Immediate (first hour — clinic decision to start ASM):**
- **Choose an ASM based on seizure type and patient factors.**
  - **Focal seizures:** Lamotrigine 25–50 mg PO daily (start low, slow titration) OR Levetiracetam 250–500 mg PO BID (no titration, but side effects).  
  - **Generalised tonic-clonic:** Valproate 500 mg PO daily (avoid in women of childbearing age) OR Lamotrigine.  
  - **Absence:** Ethosuximide 250 mg PO BID (first-line) OR Valproate.  
  - **Juvenile myoclonic:** Valproate or Levetiracetam (Lamotrigine can worsen myoclonus).  
- **Why:** Start monotherapy; aim for minimum effective dose.  
- **Pitfall:** Do **not** start phenytoin for chronic epilepsy (frequent interactions, dose-dependent kinetics) except in acute setting.

**Ward (titration and monitoring — 24-72 hours to weeks):**
- **Titrate slowly** (e.g., lamotrigine increase by 25–50 mg every 1–2 weeks) to target dose (200–400 mg/day).  
- **Monitor for side effects:** Dizziness, diplopia, rash (especially lamotrigine — if maculopapular rash, stop immediately to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome), sedation (topiramate, phenobarbital).  
- **Check drug levels** only for phenytoin, valproate, carbamazepine (therapeutic ranges: valproate 300–700 µmol/L). Levetiracetam and lamotrigine levels less useful.  
- **Patient education:**
  - Driving: First unprovoked seizure → 12-month ban (UK); second seizure → 6 months off if controlled.
  - Pregnancy planning: Folic acid 5 mg daily; avoid valproate if possible; switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam.
  - Avoid alcohol bingeing; sleep deprivation.

**Long-term (maintenance — months to years):**
- **Goal:** Seizure-free for ≥2 years on stable dose.  
- **Consider withdrawal** after 2–5 years if low risk (normal EEG, single seizure type, idiopathic). Taper over weeks to months.  
- **If refractory:** Try second monotherapy; then combination therapy; refer to epilepsy surgery (temporal lobectomy, vagus nerve stimulation).  
- **SUDEP counselling:** Take medication every day; minimise seizure triggers; night-time supervision; avoid sleep deprivation.  
- **Pitfall:** Do **not** abruptly stop ASMs — risk of status epilepticus. Do **not** ignore psychiatric comorbidities (depression, anxiety) — treat with SSRIs (e.g., citalopram) which are safe in epilepsy.

**Common drugs doses list (typical maintenance):**
- Lamotrigine: 100–400 mg/day (divided BID)
- Levetiracetam: 1,000–3,000 mg/day (BID)
- Valproate: 500–2,000 mg/day (BID)
- Carbamazepine: 600–1,200 mg/day (TID)
- Topiramate: 200–400 mg/day (BID)
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Epilepsy | Syncope | Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Seizures (PNES) | Migraine (complicated) |
|---------|----------|---------|-------------------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Provoked by** | Spontaneous, sleep deprivation | Prolonged standing, fear, valsalva | Stress, trauma, abuse | Visual aura, menstrual cycle |
| **Semiology** | Tonic-clonic, automatisms, postictal confusion | Brief (seconds) loss of tone, pallor, clonic jerks can occur (convulsive syncope) | Thrashing, pelvic thrusting, asymmetric movements, closed eyes, long duration (>2 min) | Aura (visual), headache, nausea, photophobia, occasional speech disturbance |
| **Post‐ictal** | Confusion, drowsiness, headache | Rapidly lucid | Rapid return to normal or crying | Headache, fatigue |
| **EEG** | Epileptiform discharges >24h post-ictal | Normal | Normal during event (ambulatory EEG helpful) | Normal or slowing during aura |
| **Key differentiator** | Clear epileptiform activity, tongue bite (lateral), urinary incontinence | Loss of consciousness <30 seconds, often triggered by pain/orthostasis | Eyes closed during attack, resistance to eye opening, asynchronous limb movements | Visual aura evolving into headache; no loss of consciousness |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Status epilepticus** | Seizure lasting >5 min or repeated without recovery | Ongoing convulsions, fever, tachycardia, acidosis, rhabdomyolysis | Midazolam 10 mg buccal or IV lorazepam 4 mg; call for emergency, start phenytoin infusion, escalate to ICU |
| **SUDEP** | Sudden death in a person with epilepsy, often nocturnal | Found in bed, no other cause; risk factors: uncontrolled seizures, nocturnal seizures, male, non-adherence | Prevent: educate about adherence, night-time supervision, avoid polytherapy if possible |
| **Side effects of ASMs** | Lamotrigine → rash (SJS); valproate → tremor, hepatotoxicity, PCOs; levetiracetam → irritability, psychosis | Rash, jaundice, weight gain, behavioural changes | Stop lamotrigine if rash, check LFTs, adjust dose, switch drug |
| **Postictal psychosis** | After cluster of seizures – confusion, delusions, hallucinations | Lucid interval of 1–3 days then psychosis; risk of injury | Olanzapine 5–10 mg PRN, avoid benzodiazepines; adjust ASM regimen |
| **Falls & injuries** | During seizures – fractures, head trauma | Contusions, lacerations, dental injury | Safety helmets, home modifications, osteoporosis screening if on enzyme-inducing ASMs (e.g., phenytoin) |

**Late presentation pattern:** A patient with known epilepsy presents with confusion and mild fever for 2 days, then becomes unresponsive with subtle rhythmic twitching of face – this is **non-convulsive status epilepticus** requiring urgent EEG.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **"LICKS"** for side effects of phenytoin: L (lethargy, nystagmus) I (intoxication: ataxia) C (coarsening of facies) K (K deficiency: osteomalacia) S (stevens-johnson rash)
- **"START"** for first-line AEDs by seizure type:  
  - S: Simple/complex focal → **Lamotrigine** or **Levetiracetam**  
  - T: Tonic-clonic → **Valproate** or **Lamotrigine**  
  - A: Absence → **Ethosuximide**  
  - R: Refractory → add second drug → refer  
  - T: Triggers → avoid sleep deprivation, alcohol, flashing lights
- **"S-SAFE"** for SUDEP risk factors: S (seizure frequency>1/month), S (structural brain lesion), A (alcohol use), F (forget meds – non-adherence), E (evening/night-time seizures)

**Clinical pearls:**
- A **tongue bite** (especially lateral) is highly specific for generalised tonic-clonic seizure (not syncope).
- **Postictal Todd's paresis** (transient focal weakness) suggests seizure origin in contralateral motor cortex.
- **Absence seizures** are provoked by hyperventilation – ask patient to hyperventilate for 3 min in clinic (if safe) to trigger.
- **Levetiracetam** causes irritability/aggression in up to 10% – consider switch to brivaracetam if problematic.
- **Valproate** is the most effective drug for juvenile myoclonic epilepsy but absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to high teratogenicity (neural tube defects, lower IQ).
- **Lamotrigine** levels drop during pregnancy – dose may need to be doubled in second/third trimester.
- **Always order an ECG** in new-onset epilepsy – long QT syndrome can mimic convulsive syncope.

**Common mistakes:**
- Starting a benzodiazepine alone for chronic epilepsy (tolerance develops, withdrawal seizures).
- Withdrawing an AED after first seizure without assessing risk (only half of patients remain seizure-free after first event).
- Misinterpreting convulsive syncope as epilepsy – look for prodrome of lightheadedness, pallor, rapid recovery.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / neurologist on-call / ICU:**
- **Status epilepticus** (seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery) – call emergency, crash team, neuro.
- **First unprovoked seizure with abnormal mental status** – need urgent CT/MRI, infection workup, possibly LP.
- **New-onset seizures in pregnancy** – call obstetric team and neurologist (may be eclampsia or ASM toxicity).
- **Severe cutaneous adverse reaction** (widespread rash, mucosal involvement, blistering) – stop lamotrigine/carbamazepine/phenytoin, call dermatology, monitor for SJS.
- **Seizure with fever and nuchal rigidity** – suspect meningitis/encephalitis – start IV antibiotics/acyclovir, call ICU.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2:** If ≥5 (any red parameter) → escalate to outreach; if ≥7 → call crash team.
- **Lactate:** >4 mmol/L after a first seizure suggests prolonged convulsion or hypoxia – high risk for status.
- **GCS:** Persistently GCS <9 postictally for >30 minutes → call anaesthetics for airway protection and stat EEG.
- **Prolactin:** >1000 mIU/L (3x upper limit) strongly supports epileptic seizure (but not definitive).

**This is your study-mode entry. Revise it, reference it, and keep it in your personal knowledge bank.**

## Epilepsy — Chronic Management
**One-liner:** Recurrent unprovoked seizures require sustained antiseizure therapy; goal is seizure freedom with minimal side effects — affects ~1% of the population.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents to neurology clinic. She had her first tonic-clonic seizure six months ago, then a second last week — both occurring during sleep, witnessed by her partner. She is otherwise healthy, no fever, no head trauma. EEG shows epileptiform discharges over the right temporal lobe. MRI brain is normal. She is now starting lamotrigine. Her main concern: "Can I drive? What about pregnancy?"

**Dissect the vignette:**
- Two unprovoked seizures → diagnosis of epilepsy (≥2 seizures >24 hours apart)
- Occurring during sleep → suggests focal seizure with secondary generalisation (often temporal lobe)
- Normal MRI → no structural lesion; likely genetic/idiopathic
- EEG with temporal spikes → supports focal epilepsy
- Young woman → teratogenicity concerns guide AED choice

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Subtle focal aware seizures:** Brief déjà vu, epigastric rising sensation, or odd smell — often dismissed as "daydreaming" or anxiety
- **Nocturnal only:** Patient unaware; partner reports bizarre movements, confused arousals, or bed-wetting
- **Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy:** Morning myoclonus (jerks) in teens, often triggered by sleep deprivation — misdiagnosed as clumsiness

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Definition:** Epilepsy is a brain disorder characterised by an enduring predisposition to generate epileptic seizures, defined clinically by at least two unprovoked seizures >24 hours apart, or one unprovoked seizure with >60% recurrence risk (e.g., abnormal EEG, structural lesion). Chronic management aims to suppress seizures through long-term antiseizure medication (ASM), lifestyle modification, and monitoring for drug side effects and comorbidities.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** ~50 million people worldwide; 5–10% of the population will have a seizure in their lifetime, but only 1% develop epilepsy.
- **Stakes:** Uncontrolled seizures impair quality of life, increase risk of sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) >20-fold, and cause cognitive decline, injury, and psychiatric comorbidity.
- **Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosis (syncope, PNES), over/under-titration of ASMs, failure to address pregnancy planning, driving restrictions, and adherence issues.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The story:** Epilepsy results from an imbalance between excitatory (glutamate) and inhibitory (GABA) neurotransmission, often with sodium/potassium channel dysfunction. A seizure begins when a group of hyperexcitable neurons (seizure focus) synchronously depolarise and recruit surrounding tissue. In chronic epilepsy, this propensity persists due to:
- Genetic channelopathies (e.g., SCN1A mutations → Dravet syndrome)
- Scar tissue (e.g., mesial temporal sclerosis, post-stroke gliosis)
- Excitoxicity-induced remodelling (long-term potentiation)

**Why signs happen:**
- **Tonic-clonic:** Synchronous discharge → rigidity (tonic) → alternating contraction/relaxation (clonic) due to recovery cycles
- **Focal aware:** Localised cortical activation — temporal lobe → déjà vu, olfactory hallucination; motor cortex → focal jerking
- **Absence:** Thalamocortical dysfunction → brief loss of awareness without postictal confusion
- **Postictal confusion:** Exhaustion of inhibitory interneurons, neurotransmitter depletion, hyperventilation

**Connect to pattern cues:** The vignette's nocturnal seizures suggest temporal lobe origin because sleep promotes hippocampal excitability; normal MRI implies no macroscopic lesion, so pathophysiology is likely microstructural or genetic.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Cause Category | Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------------|----------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic/Genetic** | Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, childhood absence, SCN1A mutations | Onset in childhood/adolescence; normal imaging; family history |
| **Structural** | Mesial temporal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, brain tumour, stroke | Focal seizure semiology; abnormal MRI; older age at onset |
| **Infectious** | Neurocysticercosis, meningitis, encephalitis (e.g., HSV) | Fever, headache, immunocompromise; eosinophilia; travel history |
| **Autoimmune** | Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, LGI1 antibody | Subacute onset; psychiatric features, faciobrachial dystonic seizures, hyponatremia (LGI1) |
| **Metabolic/ Toxic** | Hyponatremia, hypoglycaemia, alcohol withdrawal, drug toxicity (TCAs, tramadol) | Seizure after intoxication/withdrawal; abnormal labs; acute onset |
| **Trauma** | Post-traumatic epilepsy (esp. penetrating TBI) | History of head injury; frontal lobe signs; contusion on MRI |

**Most common cause:** Despite extensive workup, ~60% remain **idiopathic** (no identifiable cause) — especially in young adults with generalised epilepsy.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria (ILAE 2017):**  
- At least two unprovoked seizures >24 hours apart  
or  
- One unprovoked seizure with ≥60% probability of recurrence (e.g., epileptiform EEG, structural lesion on MRI)

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **EEG** | Interictal epileptiform discharges (sharp waves, spikes, polyspikes), background slowing | Confirm epilepsy, localise focus (focal vs generalised) | A single normal EEG does NOT rule out epilepsy; sleep-deprived EEG increases yield. |
| **MRI brain (3T, epilepsy protocol)** | Hippocampal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, tumour, stroke | Exclude structural causes | Normal MRI ≠ no epilepsy; avoid CT unless emergency. |
| **Bloods** | Glucose, Na, Ca, Mg, LFTs, U&Es, prolactin (post-ictal) | Rule out provoked seizure | Prolactin elevated >2x baseline within 20 min of seizure (supportive, not diagnostic) |
| **ECG** | Long QT, pre-excitation, arrhythmia | Differentiate syncope from seizure | Consider 12-lead routinely; tilt-table testing if uncertain. |

**Single finding that rules out:** Absence of any clinical seizure and normal EEG/imaging after full workup → consider non-epileptic attack disorder.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
(Note: "Immediate" here refers to initiating ASM after diagnosis; "Ward" refers to titration/monitoring in outpatient clinic; "Long-term" is maintenance and lifestyle.)

**Immediate (first hour — clinic decision to start ASM):**
- **Choose an ASM based on seizure type and patient factors.**
  - **Focal seizures:** Lamotrigine 25–50 mg PO daily (start low, slow titration) OR Levetiracetam 250–500 mg PO BID (no titration, but side effects).  
  - **Generalised tonic-clonic:** Valproate 500 mg PO daily (avoid in women of childbearing age) OR Lamotrigine.  
  - **Absence:** Ethosuximide 250 mg PO BID (first-line) OR Valproate.  
  - **Juvenile myoclonic:** Valproate or Levetiracetam (Lamotrigine can worsen myoclonus).  
- **Why:** Start monotherapy; aim for minimum effective dose.  
- **Pitfall:** Do **not** start phenytoin for chronic epilepsy (frequent interactions, dose-dependent kinetics) except in acute setting.

**Ward (titration and monitoring — 24-72 hours to weeks):**
- **Titrate slowly** (e.g., lamotrigine increase by 25–50 mg every 1–2 weeks) to target dose (200–400 mg/day).  
- **Monitor for side effects:** Dizziness, diplopia, rash (especially lamotrigine — if maculopapular rash, stop immediately to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome), sedation (topiramate, phenobarbital).  
- **Check drug levels** only for phenytoin, valproate, carbamazepine (therapeutic ranges: valproate 300–700 µmol/L). Levetiracetam and lamotrigine levels less useful.  
- **Patient education:**
  - Driving: First unprovoked seizure → 12-month ban (UK); second seizure → 6 months off if controlled.
  - Pregnancy planning: Folic acid 5 mg daily; avoid valproate if possible; switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam.
  - Avoid alcohol bingeing; sleep deprivation.

**Long-term (maintenance — months to years):**
- **Goal:** Seizure-free for ≥2 years on stable dose.  
- **Consider withdrawal** after 2–5 years if low risk (normal EEG, single seizure type, idiopathic). Taper over weeks to months.  
- **If refractory:** Try second monotherapy; then combination therapy; refer to epilepsy surgery (temporal lobectomy, vagus nerve stimulation).  
- **SUDEP counselling:** Take medication every day; minimise seizure triggers; night-time supervision; avoid sleep deprivation.  
- **Pitfall:** Do **not** abruptly stop ASMs — risk of status epilepticus. Do **not** ignore psychiatric comorbidities (depression, anxiety) — treat with SSRIs (e.g., citalopram) which are safe in epilepsy.

**Common drugs doses list (typical maintenance):**
- Lamotrigine: 100–400 mg/day (divided BID)
- Levetiracetam: 1,000–3,000 mg/day (BID)
- Valproate: 500–2,000 mg/day (BID)
- Carbamazepine: 600–1,200 mg/day (TID)
- Topiramate: 200–400 mg/day (BID)

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Epilepsy | Syncope | Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Seizures (PNES) | Migraine (complicated) |
|---------|----------|---------|-------------------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Provoked by** | Spontaneous, sleep deprivation | Prolonged standing, fear, valsalva | Stress, trauma, abuse | Visual aura, menstrual cycle |
| **Semiology** | Tonic-clonic, automatisms, postictal confusion | Brief (seconds) loss of tone, pallor, clonic jerks can occur (convulsive syncope) | Thrashing, pelvic thrusting, asymmetric movements, closed eyes, long duration (>2 min) | Aura (visual), headache, nausea, photophobia, occasional speech disturbance |
| **Post‐ictal** | Confusion, drowsiness, headache | Rapidly lucid | Rapid return to normal or crying | Headache, fatigue |
| **EEG** | Epileptiform discharges >24h post-ictal | Normal | Normal during event (ambulatory EEG helpful) | Normal or slowing during aura |
| **Key differentiator** | Clear epileptiform activity, tongue bite (lateral), urinary incontinence | Loss of consciousness <30 seconds, often triggered by pain/orthostasis | Eyes closed during attack, resistance to eye opening, asynchronous limb movements | Visual aura evolving into headache; no loss of consciousness |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Status epilepticus** | Seizure lasting >5 min or repeated without recovery | Ongoing convulsions, fever, tachycardia, acidosis, rhabdomyolysis | Midazolam 10 mg buccal or IV lorazepam 4 mg; call for emergency, start phenytoin infusion, escalate to ICU |
| **SUDEP** | Sudden death in a person with epilepsy, often nocturnal | Found in bed, no other cause; risk factors: uncontrolled seizures, nocturnal seizures, male, non-adherence | Prevent: educate about adherence, night-time supervision, avoid polytherapy if possible |
| **Side effects of ASMs** | Lamotrigine → rash (SJS); valproate → tremor, hepatotoxicity, PCOs; levetiracetam → irritability, psychosis | Rash, jaundice, weight gain, behavioural changes | Stop lamotrigine if rash, check LFTs, adjust dose, switch drug |
| **Postictal psychosis** | After cluster of seizures – confusion, delusions, hallucinations | Lucid interval of 1–3 days then psychosis; risk of injury | Olanzapine 5–10 mg PRN, avoid benzodiazepines; adjust ASM regimen |
| **Falls & injuries** | During seizures – fractures, head trauma | Contusions, lacerations, dental injury | Safety helmets, home modifications, osteoporosis screening if on enzyme-inducing ASMs (e.g., phenytoin) |

**Late presentation pattern:** A patient with known epilepsy presents with confusion and mild fever for 2 days, then becomes unresponsive with subtle rhythmic twitching of face – this is **non-convulsive status epilepticus** requiring urgent EEG.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
- **"LICKS"** for side effects of phenytoin: L (lethargy, nystagmus) I (intoxication: ataxia) C (coarsening of facies) K (K deficiency: osteomalacia) S (stevens-johnson rash)
- **"START"** for first-line AEDs by seizure type:  
  - S: Simple/complex focal → **Lamotrigine** or **Levetiracetam**  
  - T: Tonic-clonic → **Valproate** or **Lamotrigine**  
  - A: Absence → **Ethosuximide**  
  - R: Refractory → add second drug → refer  
  - T: Triggers → avoid sleep deprivation, alcohol, flashing lights
- **"S-SAFE"** for SUDEP risk factors: S (seizure frequency>1/month), S (structural brain lesion), A (alcohol use), F (forget meds – non-adherence), E (evening/night-time seizures)

**Clinical pearls:**
- A **tongue bite** (especially lateral) is highly specific for generalised tonic-clonic seizure (not syncope).
- **Postictal Todd's paresis** (transient focal weakness) suggests seizure origin in contralateral motor cortex.
- **Absence seizures** are provoked by hyperventilation – ask patient to hyperventilate for 3 min in clinic (if safe) to trigger.
- **Levetiracetam** causes irritability/aggression in up to 10% – consider switch to brivaracetam if problematic.
- **Valproate** is the most effective drug for juvenile myoclonic epilepsy but absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to high teratogenicity (neural tube defects, lower IQ).
- **Lamotrigine** levels drop during pregnancy – dose may need to be doubled in second/third trimester.
- **Always order an ECG** in new-onset epilepsy – long QT syndrome can mimic convulsive syncope.

**Common mistakes:**
- Starting a benzodiazepine alone for chronic epilepsy (tolerance develops, withdrawal seizures).
- Withdrawing an AED after first seizure without assessing risk (only half of patients remain seizure-free after first event).
- Misinterpreting convulsive syncope as epilepsy – look for prodrome of lightheadedness, pallor, rapid recovery.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call senior / neurologist on-call / ICU:**
- **Status epilepticus** (seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery) – call emergency, crash team, neuro.
- **First unprovoked seizure with abnormal mental status** – need urgent CT/MRI, infection workup, possibly LP.
- **New-onset seizures in pregnancy** – call obstetric team and neurologist (may be eclampsia or ASM toxicity).
- **Severe cutaneous adverse reaction** (widespread rash, mucosal involvement, blistering) – stop lamotrigine/carbamazepine/phenytoin, call dermatology, monitor for SJS.
- **Seizure with fever and nuchal rigidity** – suspect meningitis/encephalitis – start IV antibiotics/acyclovir, call ICU.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2:** If ≥5 (any red parameter) → escalate to outreach; if ≥7 → call crash team.
- **Lactate:** >4 mmol/L after a first seizure suggests prolonged convulsion or hypoxia – high risk for status.
- **GCS:** Persistently GCS <9 postictally for >30 minutes → call anaesthetics for airway protection and stat EEG.
- **Prolactin:** >1000 mIU/L (3x upper limit) strongly supports epileptic seizure (but not definitive).
